<59>Sadistic Game Design
<60>Complete the game with following settings, accomplishing the following deeds: 1) Core difficulty or above; 2) Party's speed depends on the weight you're carrying; 3) No auto level-up for PC or companions; 4) No retrain; 5) No more than 75 rests total; 6) Find and kill all optional bosses in the earliest chapter possible; 7) Defeat Khorramzadeh's army in Crusade mode; 8) Develop all four Crusade stats to their maximum potential; 9) Complete your mythic transformation.

<204>Immunity to Repurpose
<205>The character is lying on the ground. A prone creature cannot {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g}. <206>Prone
<207>The secret knowledge the Storyteller shared with you increases your caster level by 2 and provides a +2 insight bonus on saving throws against arcane spells. When making a Knowledge or Lore check you always roll twice and choose the best result.
<208>Storyteller's Knowledge

<210>Demonic augmented organs grant the wearer a +2 natural armor enhancement {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}Armor Class{/g}, +2 inherent bonus to {g|Encyclopedia:Check}checks{/g} made to overcome {g|Encyclopedia:Spell_Resistance}spell resistance{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Concentration}Concentration checks{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}Attack{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}Damage rolls{/g}, and all {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g}. However, whenever your {g|Encyclopedia:HP}HP{/g} falls below 20%, you enter a frenzy and attack the nearest creature until you die or combat ends.
<211>The gates are screaming. All creatures in a 50-foot area must pass a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Fortitude saving throw{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} 35) or suffer 170 {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:HP}HP{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d4{/g} damage to all {g|Encyclopedia:Ability_Scores}ability scores{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:Strength}Strength{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Dexterity}Dexterity{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Constitution{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Intelligence}Intelligence{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wisdom{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Charisma{/g}). Successful saving throw negates damage.
<212>Whenever a creature enters the toxic mist, it suffers 1d4 damage, and its movement speed is halved. Unless the creature immediately leaves the mist, each next round it has to make a successful Fortitude saving throw (DC 20) or suffer 3d10 damage.

<362>First Memories
<363>This feature grants you the following benefits depending on its rank:<br>1st — a +5 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>2nd — a +2 bonus on attack rolls and a +5 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>3rd — Moderate Fortification effect; a +2 bonus on attack rolls and a +5 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>4th — Moderate Fortification effect; a +2 bonus on attack rolls and a +10 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>5th — Moderate Fortification effect; a +4 bonus on attack rolls and a +10 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>6th — Improved Moderate Fortification effect (60%); a +4 bonus on attack rolls and a +10 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>7th — Improved Moderate Fortification effect (60%); a +4 bonus on attack rolls and a +15 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>8th — Improved Moderate Fortification effect (60%); a +6 bonus on attack rolls and a +15 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>9th — Heavy Fortification effect; a +6 bonus on attack rolls and a +15 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies<br>10th — Heavy Fortification effect; a +6 bonus on attack rolls and a +20 bonus on damage rolls against lawful enemies
<364>Accord of Chaos
<365>You gain a +2 luck bonus on saving throws against the Anomaly Distortion effect. There is also a 33% chance that Distortion will not occur.
<366>Anomaly Eradicator
<367>This power grants you the following benefits depending on its rank:<br>1st — a +2 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect<br>2nd — a +2 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect and a +2 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>3rd — Spell resistance 25, a +2 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +2 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>4th — Spell resistance 25, a +4 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +2 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>5th — Spell resistance 25, a +4 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +4 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>6th — Spell resistance 35, a +4 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +4 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>7th — Spell resistance 35, a +6 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +4 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>8th — Spell resistance 35, a +6 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +6 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>9th — Spell resistance 40, a +6 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +6 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies<br>10th — Spell resistance 40, a +8 bonus on saving throws against Anomaly Distortion effect, and a +6 bonus to AC against chaotic enemies
<368>Accord of Law
<369>Every rank of this power grants you a +2 bonus to all ability scores.
<370>Accord of Balance
<371>Those who suffer from the wrath of the Colorless Lord lose all their spell resistance as well as spell immunity (including Magic Nullification) and also have to roll twice on all saving throws taking the worst result.
<372>Wrath of the Colorless Lord
<373>Those who are filled with primordial fear lose their immunity to mind-affecting spells and conditions.
<374>Primordial Fear
<375>An affected ally receives a +2 alchemical {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g} against mind-affecting effects.<br>You can gain Clarifying Incense up to 3 times. Its effects stack.
<376>A 1st-level incense synthesizer can use his ability to create incense fog in a 15-foot area around him to aid his allies (including himself), improving their combat abilities. An affected ally receives a +1 alchemical {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} and weapon {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g}.
<377>An affected ally receives additional a +1 alchemical {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} and weapon {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g}.<br>You can gain Improved Incense up to 2 times. Its effects stack.
<378>Incense fog area increases to 30 feet around incense synthesizer.
<379>Alchemical Nauseated
<380>An affected ally receives a +2 alchemical {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g} against poisons.<br>You can gain Neutralizing Incense up to 3 times. Its effects stack.
<381>An affected ally receives a +2 alchemical {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g} against fear effects.<br>You can gain Rallying Incense up to 3 times. Its effects stack.
<382>Any undead creatures and evil outsiders who enter the area of incense fog must succeed at a Fortitude save or become alchemically sickened for 1d4 rounds. The DC of this save is equal to 10 + half the alchemist's level + the alchemist's Intelligence modifier.
<383>Any undead creatures and evil outsiders who enter the area of incense fog must succeed at a Fortitude save or become alchemically nauseated and alchemically sickened for 1d4 rounds. If the save succeeds, the opponent is only be alchemical sickened for 1d4 rounds. The DC of this save is equal to 10 + half the alchemist's level + the alchemist's Intelligence modifier.
<384>Alchemically Sickened
<385>An affected ally receives concealment (20% miss chance).
<386>An affected enemy receives a -2 alchemical penalty on {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g} against poisons.<br>You can gain Toxic Incense up to 3 times. Its effects stack.
<387>An affected enemy receives a -2 alchemical penalty on {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g} against mind-affecting effects.<br>You can gain Unsettling Fog up to 3 times. Its effects stack.
<388>A incense synthesizer learns a number of alchemical tricks, called incenses, which grant ally powers or weaken foes. At 3rd level, a incense synthesizer learns one incense. At 6th, 9th, 12th, 15th and 18th level, the incense synthesizer learns new incense. A incense synthesizer cannot select a incense more than once unless noted otherwise.
<389>Incense
<390>At 2nd level, and then again every 2 levels thereafter, an alchemist makes an incredible alchemical discovery. At 20th level, the alchemist makes an additional discovery. Unless otherwise noted, an alchemist cannot select an individual discovery more than once. Some discoveries can only be made if the alchemist has met certain prerequisites first, such as uncovering other discoveries. Only one such discovery can be applied to an individual bomb. The {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of any {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throw{/g} called for by a discovery is equal to 10 + 1/2 the alchemist's level + the alchemist's {g|Encyclopedia:Intelligence}Intelligence{/g} modifier.
<391>This ability allows the character to react to a danger faster than others. Thanks to it, they can evade a {g|Encyclopedia:Penalty}penalty{/g} to their {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}armor class{/g} in some situations, and become immune to some of the enemies' tactical advantages.
<392>When unarmored and not wielding a shield, the instinctual warrior adds his {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wisdom{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} (if any) to his {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:CMD}CMD{/g}. In addition, an instinctual warrior gains a +1 bonus to AC and CMD at 4th level. This bonus increases by 1 for every four barbarian levels thereafter, up to a maximum of +5 at 20th level.
<393>The extremely developed sixth sense of the instinctual warrior allows him to have a blindsense to a range of 30 feet, blindsight to a range of 15 feet.
<394>At 2nd level, a dirge bard gains a {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} equal to half his bard level on {g|Encyclopedia:Lore_Religion}Lore (religion){/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}checks{/g}. A dirge bard may use mind-affecting {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spells{/g} to affect undead as if they were living creatures, undead creatures are treated as humanoids for the purposes of determining which spells affect them. In addition, he adds the following necromantic spells to his list of spells known: cause fear at 2nd level; command undead at 6th level; fear at 10th level; waves of fatigue at 14th level; banshee blast at 18th level.
<395>At 13th level, inspire tranquility also grants blindsense with a 10-foot range.
<396>To treat an affliction means to tend to a single diseased or poisoned character. Make a {g|Encyclopedia:Lore_Religion}Lore (religion){/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g}. If your check exceeds the {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of the affliction, the affliction is removed. You can treat each character only once per day. You need a first aid kit to use this ability.
<397>The demon lord of portals and riddles, Areshkagal appears as a faceless six-legged female sphinx with midnight blue fur and pale flesh. She torments her worshipers and her prisoners alike with unfair riddles until they go mad and renounce the very reality of their existence. Whispers hold that her true face is too hideous for even the Abyss to bear, but that for brief moments she can reveal it to strike viewers insane or dead.<br>Domains: Air, Chaos, Evil, Trickery.<br>Favored Weapon: Sickle
<398>The carrion-stenched Kabriri, Him Who Gnaws, is the demon lord of ghouls, graves, and secrets kept by the dead. According to legend, Kabriri is the reborn form of the first humanoid to devour his kin in life. Kabriri is predominantly worshiped by the cannibalistic ghouls that dwell in the hidden warrens beneath Golarion's surface. His followers enact bloody rituals in his honour beneath the gibbous moon, dancing and feasting beneath the night sky.<br>Domains: Death, Chaos, Evil, Knowledge.<br>Favored Weapon: Flail
<399>Leader of a small cult of undead, created by h{mf|is|er} own hands, {name} became famous as the indomitable commander of the Fifth Mendevian Crusade against the Worldwound. Determined to destroy the forces of the Abyss at any cost, {mf||s}he ventured on the path of the L{mf|ord|ady} of Death. For millennia, powerful necromancers have dreamed of a power that could rival the divine. They have created cults in their own names, and attracted many followers. Thanks to h{mf|is|er} legendary powers, {name} has managed to achieve much greater success than the others: {mf||s}he has acquired a talent to bestow divine spells upon h{mf|is|er} followers. From that moment, the undead created by h{mf|im|er} needed no other deities to cast spells.<br>Domains: Death, Evil, Strength, War.
<400>Pulura, the mistress of the stars and the aurora, is an angel empyreal lord, the patron of those travelers that become homesick, lost, or injured in the snowy wastes of the far north. She often appears dancing amid the aurora borealis in the skies of far northern lands, and legend has it that her extraordinary beauty will burn any mortals who dare approach her too closely. Pulura is worshiped by denizens of the far north who believe that the aurora is an omen of her good deeds. Sailors worship her as the keeper of the constellations which mortals navigate their world, lighting the way so that those who travel or explore can always find their way home. She was also worshiped as a major deity in the lost Kellid realm of Sarkoris before it fell to the demons of the Worldwound.<br>Domains: Air, Good, Chaos, Weather<br>Favored Weapon: Sling Staff

At 7th level and every 3 levels thereafter, this increases by 1 (to a maximum of {g|Encyclopedia:Fast_Healing}fast healing{/g} 6 at 19th level).

<409>The bloodrager's source of internal power grants him the ability to bloodrage.<br>At 1st level, a bloodrager can bloodrage for a number of {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}rounds{/g} per day equal to 4 + his {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Constitution{/g} modifier. At each level after 1st, he can bloodrage for 2 additional rounds per day. Temporary increases to Constitution (such as those gained from bloodraging or {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spells{/g} like bear's endurance) don't increase the total number of rounds that a bloodrager can bloodrage per day. The total number of rounds of bloodrage per day is renewed after resting for 8 hours, although these hours need not be consecutive.<br>While in a bloodrage, a bloodrager gains a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on {g|Encyclopedia:MeleeAttack}melee attack{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}rolls{/g}, melee {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g}, thrown weapon damage rolls, and Will {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}saving throws{/g}. In addition, he takes a -2 {g|Encyclopedia:Penalty}penalty{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}Armor Class{/g}. He also gains 2 temporary {g|Encyclopedia:HP}hit points{/g} per {g|Encyclopedia:Hit_Dice}Hit Die{/g}. These temporary hit points are lost first when a character takes damage, disappear when the bloodrage ends, and are not replenished if the bloodrager enters a bloodrage again within 1 minute of his previous bloodrage.<br>If a bloodrager falls {g|Encyclopedia:Injury_Death}unconscious{/g}, his bloodrage immediately ends, placing him in peril of death.<br>Bloodrage counts as the barbarian's rage class feature for the purpose of {g|Encyclopedia:Feat}feat{/g} prerequisites, feat abilities, magic item abilities, and spell effects.
<410>At 9th level, the angelfire apostle becomes a direct conduit for the righteous power of his deity. Whenever the angelfire apostle casts a {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g}, for 1 {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} he can unleash a blast of flames as a {g|Encyclopedia:Swift_Action}swift action{/g} by expending one use of his channel energy ability. The flames deal {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d4{/g} points of {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} per spell level of the highest level spell he can cast to all enemies in a 20-foot radius. Half of the damage is {g|Encyclopedia:Energy_Damage}fire damage{/g}, and half is raw divine power not subject to fire resistance or immunity. Any creature in the area can halve the damage with a successful {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} 10 + half angelfire apostle level + {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Charisma{/g} modifier).

<430>This ability allows the Hellknight to select one of the following domains: Artifice, Glory, Knowledge, Law, Magic, Nobility, Protection, Rune, Strength, Travel, or War. The Hellknight gains the granted powers of that domain, treating his Hellknight levels as cleric levels to determine what domain abilities he has access to.
<431>Hellknights of the Chain value social order and responsibility, seeking to root out anarchists and fugitives. They have reputations as relentless hunters who drag lawbreakers away in chains. These Hellknights keep meticulous count of the number of criminals they've brought to justice; they never abandon the pursuit of a fugitive, and they believe everyone has a specific, crucial role to play in society.<br>At 1st level you gain a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:CMB}CMB{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:CMD}CMD{/g}. This bonus increases by +1 at 5th and 9th level.
<432>Order of the Gate Hellknights seek to curb and prevent lawlessness through magic and manipulation. They have reputations as mysterious, emotionless, and sometimes cruel magic-users. Many are convinced that the Order of the Gate's Hellknights are spying on them or already know their secrets. Sometimes this fear is true, as the order's members prefer preventing crime to punishing it and often use manipulation, information-gathering, and spying to further their agenda.<br>While a Hellknight of the Gate wears armor, he reduces the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell_Fail_Chance}arcane spell failure chance{/g} by 5% at 1st level, 10% at 5th and 20% at 9th level.
<433>Order of the Godclaw Hellknights enforce law with a crusader's zeal, going where they're needed most in the fight against the forces of chaos. They know no bounds or borders in the quest for absolute order. To Hellknights of the Godclaw, the cause of order is a holy mandate for which they will travel far to battle chaos in lands that know little of civilization. To others, these Hellknights' faith is likely inexplicable — some believe the members of the order are experts in all five individual faiths or even that they are heretics.<br>At 1st level you gain an aura which you can use 3 times per day. It lasts 1 minute and grants all allies in a 30-foot radius a +1 morale {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g} against chaotic enemies. This bonus increases by +1 at 5th and 9th level.
<434>Order of the Nail Hellknights idealize civilized societies and seek to unite all people in advancing that cultural baseline. In fact, the Order of the Nail's members preach that anyone who doesn't embrace their own culture is a criminal and heathen who must be destroyed. The order's members idealize the lifestyles and challenges of frontier settlers, no matter the displacement, indignation, or cruelty they might visit on those who were there before.<br>At 1st level, a hellknight selects a creature type from the ranger favored enemies list. He gets a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on weapon {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g} against them.<br>At 5th level and 9th level this bonus increases by additional +2.
<435>Hellknights of the Order of the Pyre seek to combat outlandish faiths and beliefs, preventing them from gaining purchase in the Inner Sea region. They are cult hunters first and foremost, but also fight to repress other philosophies they deem dangerous. These Hellknights hate and vocally condemn cultists, priests of rare religions, and followers of mystical orders.<br>At 1st level, whenever you receive {g|Encyclopedia:Energy_Damage}fire damage{/g}, you gain fast {g|Encyclopedia:Healing}healing{/g} 5 for the number of {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}rounds{/g} equal to your level in this prestige class. At 5th level you gain {g|Encyclopedia:Fast_Healing}fast healing{/g} 10 and at 9th level you gain fast healing 15 instead.
<436>Order of the Rack Hellknights hate wastefulness of both thought and deed. They crush pointless dreams, end rebellions, and trample frivolous or dangerous inventions. They also closely observe individuals, seeking hints of destructive ambitions and wasteful vices, and are skilled at destroying seditious writing and strange inventions.<br>Three times per day as a {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g} you can target a single creature. <437>Hellknights of the Scourge seek out corruption within order and laws not fit for the lawful. They question and seek to determine the difference between what is legal and what is for the greater good — owing allegiance to no one nation or ruler. <462>You can summon the spirit of a great warrior ancestor and allow it to possess you, becoming a mighty warrior yourself. You gain a +4 enhancement {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Strength}Strength{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Dexterity}Dexterity{/g}, and {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Constitution{/g}, and a +4 natural armor bonus to {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g}. Your {g|Encyclopedia:BAB}base attack bonus{/g} while possessed equals your oracle level (which may give you additional {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacks{/g}), and any weapon you are wielding gains Keen enchantment. You can use this ability for 1 {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} for every 2 oracle levels you possess. This duration does not need to be consecutive, but it must be spent in 1-round increments. You must be at least 11th level to select this revelation.
<463>Upon reaching 5th level, a paladin forms a divine bond with her god. This bond can take one of two forms. Once the form is chosen, it cannot be changed.<br>The first type of bond allows a paladin to form a divine bond with her weapon. As a {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g}, she can call upon the aid of a celestial spirit for 1 minute per paladin level.<br>At 5th level, this spirit grants the weapon a +1 enhancement {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g}. For every three levels beyond 5th, the weapon gains another +1 enhancement bonus, to a maximum of +6 at 20th level. These bonuses can be added to the weapon, stacking with existing weapon bonuses to a maximum of +5.<br>Alternatively, they can be used to add any of the following weapon properties: axiomatic, brilliant energy, disruption, flaming, flaming burst, holy, keen, and {g|Encyclopedia:Speed}speed{/g}. Adding these properties consumes an amount of bonus equal to the property's cost. These bonuses are added to any properties the weapon already has, but duplicate abilities do not stack.<br>A paladin can use this ability once per day at 5th level, and one additional time per day for every four levels beyond 5th, to a total of four times per day at 17th level.<br>The second type of bond allows a paladin to gain the service of an unusually {g|Encyclopedia:Intelligence}intelligent{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Strength}strong{/g}, and loyal steed to serve her in her crusade against evil. This mount is a heavy horse. This mount functions as a druid's animal companion, using the paladin's level as her effective druid level.<br>At 11th level, the mount gains the celestial creature simple template.<br>At 15th level, a paladin's mount gains {g|Encyclopedia:Spell_Resistance}spell resistance{/g} equal to the paladin's level + 11.
<464>{g|Encyclopedia:Size}Size{/g} Medium;<br>{g|Encyclopedia:Speed}Speed{/g} 30 ft.;<br>{g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g} +6 natural armor;<br>{g|Encyclopedia:Attack}Attack{/g} gore ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d8{/g});<br>{g|Encyclopedia:Ability_Scores}Ability Scores{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Strength}Str{/g} 10, {g|Encyclopedia:Dexterity}Dex{/g} 13, {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Con{/g} 11, {g|Encyclopedia:Intelligence}Int{/g} 2, {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wis{/g} 12, {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Cha{/g} 7.<br>At 7th level becomes Large, gains +3 to natural armor, gore attack becomes 2d6, gains +8 to Str, -2 to Dex, +4 Con and Powerful charge (gore).

At 9th level, a wrecker's natural {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacks{/g} count as if they were adamantine for the purpose of overcoming a construct's {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} reduction.

<471>At 1st level, a ranger selects a creature type from the ranger favored enemies list. He gets a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on weapon {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage rolls{/g} against them.<br>At 5th level and every five levels thereafter (10th, 15th, and 20th level), the ranger may select an additional favored enemy. In addition, at each such interval, the bonus against any one favored enemy (including the one just selected, if so desired) increases by +2.<br>If the ranger chooses humanoids or outsiders as a favored enemy, he must also choose an associated subtype, as indicated on the table below. If a specific creature falls into more than one category of favored enemy, the ranger's bonuses do not stack; he simply uses whichever bonus is higher.<br>Demons of Magic are the group of demons who prefer to use spells in battle. Some of the Demons of Magic are dretch, oolioddroo, and succubus.

<474>Minor Spirit — Fourth
<475>You can use dispel magic to end one ongoing {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} that has been cast on a creature.<br>You make one dispel {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d20{/g} + your {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g}) and compare that to the spell with highest caster level ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} = 11 + the spell's caster level). If successful, that spell ends. If not, compare the same result to the spell with the next highest caster level. Repeat this process until you have dispelled one spell affecting the target, or you have failed to dispel every spell.<br>At 8th level, the witch doctor can choose to lose any prepared magic {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} that is 3rd level or higher in order to spontaneously cast dispel magic or remove curse. This ability can only target a spell effect that is on an ally (including herself ). If she forfeits a magic spell higher than 3rd level, she gains a +2 sacred {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on her {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} to dispel the spell or to remove the curse for every spell level higher than 3rd that she sacrifices.
<476>Remove curse can remove all curses on an object or a creature. If the target is a creature, you must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d20{/g} + caster level) against the {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of each curse affecting the target. Success means that the curse is removed.<br>At 8th level, the witch doctor can choose to lose any prepared magic {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} that is 3rd level or higher in order to spontaneously cast dispel magic or remove curse. This ability can only target a spell effect that is on an ally (including herself ). If she forfeits a magic spell higher than 3rd level, she gains a +2 sacred {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on her {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} to dispel the spell or to remove the curse for every spell level higher than 3rd that she sacrifices.

<478>The shaman touches a willing creature (including herself) and grants a battle ward. The next time a foe makes an {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack roll{/g} against the target, the ward activates and grants a +3 deflection {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to the warded creature's {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g}. Each subsequent time she's attacked, the deflection bonus reduces by 1 (to +2 for the second time she's attacked and +1 for the third). The ward fades when the bonus is reduced to +0 or after 24 hours, whichever comes first. At 8th level, the ward's starting bonus increases to +4. At 16th level, it increases to +5. A creature affected by this hex cannot be affected by it again for 24 hours.
<479>Channeling positive energy causes a burst that {g|Encyclopedia:Healing}heals{/g} all living creatures in a 30-foot radius centered on the shaman. The amount of {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} healed is equal to {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d6{/g} points of damage plus 1d6 points of damage for every two shaman levels beyond 1st (2d6 at 3rd, 3d6 at 5th, and so on).
<480>Channeling positive energy causes a burst that {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damages{/g} all undead creatures in a 30-foot radius centered on the shaman. The amount of damage inflicted is equal to {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d6{/g} points of damage plus 1d6 points of damage for every two shaman levels beyond 1st (2d6 at 3rd, 3d6 at 5th, and so on). Creatures that take damage from channeled energy receive a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Will save{/g} to halve the damage. The {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of this save is equal to 10 + 1/2 the shaman's level + the shaman's {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Charisma{/g} modifier.
<481>The shaman gains a +4 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} on all {g|Encyclopedia:Lore_Religion}Lore (religion){/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}skill checks{/g}. <491>Call of the Wild — Beast Shape I
<492>At 10th level, the hunt caller's raging song can transform herself and her companions within 15 feet into animalistic shapes, as per beast shape I. The same transformation applies to all targets. By spending 1 {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} of raging song, the hunt caller can affect any number of companions within 15 feet for 1 hour. When in animal form, the hunt caller is treated as able to speak normally for the purpose of using raging song, but not for using other abilities that require speech (such as {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spellcasting{/g}). The skald must continue to perform the song for the remainder of the hour; otherwise, its effects end, but only 1 round of raging song is expended for that hour. Raging song and transformation effect end when battle ends.<br>At 14th level, the hunt caller can choose the effects of beast shape II. At 18th level, she can choose the effects of beast shape III.

<534>A good warpriest (or a neutral warpriest of a good deity) can channel stored spell energy into healing spells that he did not prepare ahead of time. The warpriest can expend any prepared spell that isn't an orison to cast any cure spell of the same spell level or lower. A cure spell is any spell with <bs_n1>cure<bs_n1> in its name.<br>An evil warpriest (or a neutral warpriest of an evil deity) can't convert spells to cure spells, but can convert them to inflict spells. An inflict spell is any spell with <bs_n1>inflict<bs_n1> in its name.<br>A warpriest that is neither good nor evil and whose deity is neither good nor evil chooses whether he can convert spells into either cure spells or inflict spells. Once this choice is made, it cannot be changed. This choice also determines whether the warpriest channels positive or negative energy (see Channel Energy ability description).
<535>Whenever a disenchanter gains a bonus {g|Encyclopedia:Feat}feat{/g}, he must choose from the following list: Great Fortitude, Improved Counterspell, Iron Will, Lightning Reflexes, {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}Skill{/g} Focus ({g|Encyclopedia:Knowledge_Arcana}Knowledge (arcana){/g}), or Spell Focus (abjuration). At 9th level, he adds the following feats to this list: Dispel Synergy, Improved Great Fortitude, Improved Iron Will, and Improved Lightning Reflexes. At 12th level, he adds the following feats to this list: Destructive Dispel and Ray Shield. He need not meet the prerequisites for these feats.

<585>Slithering creatures of pure chaos, proteans claim they were the masters of the multiverse before the first gods created the other planes from the pure chaos of the primeval beginnings. They seek to tear down the multiverse and restore it to its original chaotic form.

<594>Polymorph Resistance
<595>An Anomaly creature gets a +4 anomaly bonus to Constitution and a +6 anomaly bonus to Intelligence, Wisdom and Charisma ability scores. No matter who this creature was, it now gains outsider type and all protean's traits. In addition, the creature gains unique defensive abilities (chaotic mind, images of chaos) and Anomaly Distortion ability. Creature gains access to the anomaly spell. Finally, the creature gains the following feats: Spell Penetration, Greater Spell Penetration, Combat Casting, Destructive Dispel.
<596>Anomalous Spellcaster
<597>Anomaly
<598>An Anomaly creature gets a +10 anomaly bonus to Constitution, a +6 anomaly bonus to Strength, a +8 anomaly bonus to Dexterity, a +4 anomaly bonus to Intelligence, a +8 anomaly bonus to Wisdom and a +6 anomaly bonus to Charisma ability scores. It also gains a +5 Natural Armor bonus to AC. Creature's Hit Points increase by 2×CR×CR. No matter who this creature was, it now gains 5 outsider levels and protean's traits. In addition, the creature gains one of 5 unique defensive abilities and Anomaly Distortion ability. If that creature can cast spells, it also gains access to the anomaly spell. The subject is protected from all abilities and spells that gather information about the target through divination magic (such as see invisibility, thoughtsense and true seeing).

<608>Divination Immunity
<609>Whenever such creature is targeted by a spell or is caught within the area of effect of a spell, it can, as an immediate action, choose to teleport to an area adjacent to the spell's caster, effectively appearing mid-leap and aimed toward the caster. This ability takes effect regardless of whether or not the spell overcomes the spell resistance. Such creature can immediately make a full attack against the spell's caster as though pouncing. Using this ability does not provoke an attack of opportunity

<638>Petrify
<639>A creature affected by Valmallos's Adamantine Seal resembles more of a construct and less of itself, and thus gains most of the constructs' immunities.<br>It gains immunity to mind-affecting effects, disease and critical hits. It also gains a +6 sacred bonus to all ability scores, immunity to magic, Spell Turning and Hardness 30.<br>The creature's attacks always deal 50 divine damage.<br>The creature can cast the following spells: petrify, chain lightning, disintegrate, prediction of failure, zero state, freezing nothingness.

<642>Valmallos's Iron Seal

It also gains a +4 sacred bonus to all ability scores, immunity to magic and Hardness 20.<br>The creature's attacks always deal 50 divine damage.<br>The creature can cast the following spells: petrify, chain lightning, disintegrate and prediction of failure.
<644>Valmallos's Mithral Seal
<645>Valmallos has mastered the Aeon's ability to enforce rules on everything around them, giving various benefits to the allies and hindering the enemies. Once activated, this ability lasts until the combat ends, at which point all of the bonuses immediately disappear. Valmallos Gaze includes the following Enforcer Gaze effects: {g|AeonAbilityAoOGaze}Attacks of Opportunity{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityAreaEffectsGaze}Area Effects{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityAttackGaze}Attack{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityCLGaze}Caster Level{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityCombatManeuversGaze}Combat Maneuvers{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityConcentrationGaze}Concentration{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityDamageGaze}Damage{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityDCGaze}Save DC{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityDRGaze}Damage Reduction{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityEnergyShieldGaze}Energy Shield{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityHPGaze}Hit Points{/g}, {g|AeonAbilityNormalizeSizeGaze}Normalize Size{/g}.<br>Enemies can try to make a Will saving throw to resist this effect, receiving a -2 penalty on the throw if they have the Chaotic alignment. Blind Fight feat can't counter this ability.
<646>Valmallos Gaze
<647>Valmallos gains an insight bonus to {g|Encyclopedia:Armor_Class}AC{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:CMD}CMD{/g} equal to its {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wisdom{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g}.

<695>There can be up to five companions in your party. To select a single character, click on their portrait or press a corresponding key. To select multiple characters at once and give them the same order, draw a frame around them while holding down the left mouse button. To select all characters, press [{bind|SelectAllCharacters}].

This skill cannot be used if your character is not trained in it.<br>{g|Encyclopedia:Knowledge_Arcana}Knowledge (arcana){/g} represents character's knowledge about {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spells{/g}, magic items and numerous bits of magical lore.<br>Here are examples where you apply your character's Knowledge (arcana) skill to:<br>• Arcane spell scroll scribing during {g|Encyclopedia:Rest}camping{/g}.<br>• Identification of found magical items.<br>• Learn spells from spellbooks and scrolls.

Additionally for a standard {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}action{/g} the wearer can use Dark Nova ability, it deals 20 damage to the all his companions and they get Chosen of the Dark Nova effect, while all enemies in a 30-foot area receive Pray of The Chosen. Under Chosen of the Dark Nova effect companions receive +5 damage bonus against Pray of The Chosen.<br>If the wearer is of neutral alignment, the wearer can use both of abilities. However, if the wearer used one ability, the wearer can't use another ability until the {g|Encyclopedia:Rest}rest{/g}.
<860>An adventurer once made a pilgrimage around the world in an adventure, collecting catalysts and rare items. From traveling high places from climbing the tallest sacred tree, to delving the chasms of the underworld, from searching for loot within the dungeons and caverns, to climbing misty peaks of mountains slaying mighty creatures larger than his own size.<br>From his labor he managed to search and obtain rare items such as an piece of bone from a elder dragon, an alloy bar that has been blessed with holy and the unholy, a twig from the world tree that's been blessed by the elemental spirits and the druids, and the abyssal water that reflects the constellations in the starry sky that can be obtained hidden in the labyrinth deep underground, guarded by putrid fleshy monsters.<br>By the time the man had collected all the ingredients, he decided to gather a selective group of people for one last adventure with his old team to create his own legacy. Three remote areas, from melding them together in the hottest furnace inside the heart of the volcano, to the lonely, somber cold mountain condenses material without letting it fall apart in sub-zero temperatures, then lastly in tranquil lake where special waters cleanse the impurities and allow the ore to keep most of its foundations.<br>Finally, back in his workshop, he began to heat up the furnace and started to hammer on the anvil. The magic from the furnace flared and sparked off many colors, as he kept hammering the mold for many days without stopping. Each hammer reminded him of the days of adventuring and obstacles that he'd overcame. From exploring the dungeons, discovering and learning new skills along the way, sailing for new horizons, to listening to his own exploits sang by the bard, little by little he imprinted a part of himself and little remaining of his own life force unto his masterpiece, until he finally completed forging something that could truly reflect himself: the Undying Shield of the Hopebringer.
<861>All enemies in a 30-foot range have a -2 {g|Encyclopedia:Penalty}penalty{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Initiative}Initiative{/g}, all companions gain a +2 profane {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} of opportunities and {g|Encyclopedia:Critical}critical hit{/g} confirmation. Whenever a companion lands a critical hit against the enemy, the enemy must pass a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Will saving throw{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} 25) or be under sleeping effect for {g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d3{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}rounds{/g}. All curses, poisons, diseases and mind-affecting {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spells{/g} have their save DC increased by 2 both for enemies and companions.

<997>Enforcing Gaze — Normalize Size
<998>Whenever an ally makes an attack roll, skill check, or a saving throw, and gets a result equal to or less than (1 + half your mythic rank), this result instead becomes (2 + half your mythic rank). <1172>You create an extra-dimensional comfy hole with a depth of 10 feet per two {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster levels{/g} (maximum 30 feet) and a trampoline at the bottom. Since the pit extends into another dimension, the pit has no weight and does not otherwise displace the original underlying material. You can create the pit in the deck of a ship as easily as in a dungeon floor or the ground of a forest.<br>Any large or smaller creature standing in the area where you first conjured the pit must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} to not jump to safety in the pit. In addition, the edges of the pit are sloped, and any creature ending its turn on a square adjacent to the pit must make a Reflex saving throw with a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to avoid falling into it. <1173>A demon who has overcome their evil nature is such a rare occurrence that most people don't even believe in their existence. The path of ascension is long and thorny: before truly achieving virtue, a demon spends many years in spiritual search and repentance, between good and evil, every moment risking to fall back into the depths of sin. An ascending demon loses most of their demonic powers, which helps them to battle the temptation to return to their old ways, but also leaves them vulnerable to retaliation from other demons, who loathe the idea of a rebellion against evil.
<1174>Succubi, sometimes called the lust demons, are a type of demons, all female, infamous for tempting mortal souls and engendering misery and destruction through their seductive manipulations. In her natural form, a succubus resembles an incredibly beautiful and alluring human woman. Despite their comeliness, succubi possess several clearly demonic attributes, not the least of which are a pair of large, bat-like wings sprouting from their shoulders and sinuous, writhing tails. A succubus is formed from the souls of particularly lustful and rapacious evil mortals.

<1194>Toggles automatic leveling-up for party members. When on, gaining levels happens automatically, and you can monitor how the hero's skills and abilities develop. You can turn off automatic leveling-up for any companion, but then you'll have to manage their development for the remainder of the game.<br><b>Off</b> — custom leveling-up of all characters.<br><b>Companions</b> — custom leveling-up of player's character. Companions are leveled-up automatically.<br><b>All possible</b> — all characters are leveled up automatically.

<1234>Vast swathes of uncharted, unclaimed terrain metaphysically border and surround each of the planes on the Outer Sphere. Mysterious and dangerously chaotic lands untouched by the gods, these regions lack the cohesive stability present in all of the other planes. Even the brutal depths of the Abyss possess a structure that the Maelstrom does not.
<1235>Maelstrom

<1237>Inevitables

<1239>Proteans
<1240>Few names arouse as much rage among crusaders as that of Areelu Vorlesh — the Sarkorian witch who initiated the opening of the Worldwound, and later its expansion. Almost nothing is known about her life before the atrocity, except that Vorlesh was condemned for using arcane magic, which was persecuted in Sarkoris, and imprisoned in the tower of Threshold near the city of Iz. This is where Areelu Vorlesh conducted the ritual that paved the way for the demonic invasion and left her land to be tormented by the demon lord Deskari. By some mysterious means, the witch transformed herself, changing from an ordinary human into a half-demon. Over the following century, crusaders encountered Areelu Vorlesh or the results of her experiments a few times, but they learned nothing more of Deskari's henchwoman. She holds a high position in the ranks of the army of the Abyss, and bears the title of Architect of the Worldwound.
<1241>Once a rising power poised to unite its neighbors under its banner, today Numeria has fallen into decay. Here, savagery and science exist side by side. Amid the wreckage of a crashed starship from a distant world, technomancers from across Golarion flock to research and exploit the technological wonders found within the otherworldly ruins; meanwhile, tribes of primitive barbarians cling to age-old tradition and eschew technology in favor of their own raw skill and martial prowess. This is a nation where, under the auspices of technological advancement and the betterment of civilization, a select few rule a subjugated nation as despots and tyrants, placing their own power and continued reign above the well-being of their citizens.

<1388>[Draft] Show Completed Quests
<1389>Assigned characters will spend their free time sorting ingredients, boiling water, brewing a potion, and cooking food. In these cursed regions, one can rely only on the proven ingredients and recipes to increase chances of survival in places where no living creature can exist. These characters must pass a Knowledge (world) skill check against a spell or cooking recipe DC. Assigned character must know the spell to prepare a potion with it. A secondary assigned character will help to pass this check.<br>{br}On the global map, the assigned character will increase the number of ingredients gathered at resource stashes the party finds. To do so, the character must pass a Knowledge (world) check against the DC of that resource stash.
<1390>[Hold] Auto Character
<1391>Rest
<1392>This is your current level of corruption. Corruption grows with each day of rest. It has 3 levels of strength. You should try to avoid reaching corruption level 3. Corruption can grow at different rates depending on the location. Rest in a safe location to remove all corruption.
<1393>Bonuses to Knowledge (world) skill check
<1394>Bonuses to Lore (religion) skill check
<1395>Bonuses to Knowledge (arcana) or Lore (religion) skill check
<1396>Difficulty Class of a Knowledge (world) skill check for brewing potions
<1397>Difficulty Class of a Lore (religion) skill check
<1398>Difficulty Class of a Lore (religion) or Knowledge (arcana)
<1399>Assigned characters will perform sacred protective rituals to weaken the growth of corruption and ensure better rest for all group members. These characters must pass a Lore (religion) skill check against the DC of the area where you rest. A secondary assigned character will help to pass this check.<br>{br}On the global map, the assigned character will perform divine rituals to increase the speed of the party. To do so the character must pass a Lore (religion) skill check against DC depending on the area. The DC increases with each successful check until the party rests.
<1400>The enemies have caught the party off-guard
<1401>The guards have raised alarm
<1402>Assigned characters will spend their free time scribing a scroll, choosing the required ingredients, and finding the right ink and scroll paper. All the time and effort dedicated to understanding magic, whether from Arcane or Divine source, is now put to the test. The character must pass a Knowledge (arcana) or Lore (religion) skill check against a spell DC. Assigned character must know the spell to scribe a scroll with it. A secondary assigned character will help to pass this check.<br>{br}On the global map, the assigned character will increase the number of ingredients gathered at resource stashes the party finds. To do so, the character must pass a Knowledge (arcana) or Lore (religion) skill check against the DC of that resource stash.

<1441>Aspect
<1442><b>Critical issue</b>: game-breaking issues like crashes; save file corruptions; quests that can't be completed; party getting stuck; issues that block character progression, etc.<br><b>Normal issue</b>: all other issues except for critical issues.<br><b>Suggestion</b>: any ideas about how to improve the game experience.
<1443>Context
<1444>Critical
<1445>1. Describe the bug. The more details you can specify, the easier it will be for us to fix it.<br>2. Help us reproduce the bug. What steps caused the bug to occur?

<1449>Bug Report
<1450>A screenshot, your save file, and your system specs will be sent along with this bug report.
<1451>Enabling this option records your in-game keystrokes in the game log that is attached to a bug report. If you are having problems with the game's response to keyboard input, enable this option, press the problematic keys, and then submit a bug report.

<1497>As a full-{g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}action{/g}, you can use a melee weapon to deliver a coup de grace to a {g|Encyclopedia:Helpless}helpless{/g} opponent.<br>You automatically hit and score a {g|Encyclopedia:Critical}critical hit{/g}. If the defender survives the {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g}, they must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Fortitude save{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} 10 + {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacker{/g}'s base attack bonus + melee damage ability modifier + weapon's critical multiplier) or die.<br>Delivering a coup de grace provokes {g|Encyclopedia:Attack_Of_Opportunity}attacks of opportunity{/g} from {g|Encyclopedia:Threatened_Area}threatening{/g} opponents.<br>You can't deliver a coup de grace against a creature that is immune to critical hits.
<1498>Character is at the {g|Encyclopedia:Injury_Death}death{/g}'s door, and escaped a deadly blow only by sheer luck. If their {g|Encyclopedia:HP}hit points{/g} fall below their negative {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Constitution{/g} score again, then they will truly die. This condition can be removed by visiting a {g|Encyclopedia:Healing}healer{/g} at the safe spot or by Greater Restoration.
<1499>The curse inflicted by the ungrateful prisoner of the Ivory Labyrinth. Not only does it weaken its bearer, it allows the caster to sense the cursed victim from any distance.<br>The bearer gets a -4 penalty on Perception skill checks and all saving throws.
<1500>After the battle, you can dismiss area {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} effect at will. You must be out of combat and within a 100-foot range of the spell's effect. Dismissing a spell is a {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g} that does not spend spell slots and does not provoke {g|Encyclopedia:Attack_Of_Opportunity}attacks of opportunity{/g}.
<1501>You create an extra-dimensional hole with a depth of 10 feet per two {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster levels{/g} (maximum 30 feet). Since the pit extends into another dimension, the pit has no weight and does not otherwise displace the original underlying material. You can create the pit in the deck of a ship as easily as in a dungeon floor or the ground of a forest.<br>Any large or smaller creature standing in the area where you first conjured the pit must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} to jump to safety in the nearest open space. In addition, the edges of the pit are sloped, and any creature ending its turn on a square adjacent to the pit must make a Reflex saving throw with a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to avoid falling into it. Creatures subjected to an effect intended to push them into the pit (such as bull rush) do not get a saving throw to avoid falling in if they are affected by the pushing effect.<br>Creatures who fall into the pit take falling {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} as normal ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d6{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Damage_Type}bludgeoning damage{/g} per 10 feet). To climb the pit's coarse stone walls, a creature needs a successful {g|Encyclopedia:Athletics}Athletics{/g} or {g|Encyclopedia:Mobility}Mobility{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}skill check{/g} with a {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of 25. When the duration of the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} ends, creatures within the hole rise up with the bottom of the pit until they are standing on the surface over the course of a single {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g}.
<1502>You gain intuitive insight into the workings of traps. You gain an insight {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} equal to half your {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g} (maximum +10) on {g|Encyclopedia:Perception}Perception skill checks{/g} made to find traps while the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} is in effect.
<1503>You create an extra-dimensional hole with a depth of 10 feet per two {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster levels{/g} (maximum 50 feet). The pit is lined with wickedly sharp spikes along its bottom and walls.<br>Any large or smaller creature standing in the area where you first conjured the pit must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} to jump to safety in the nearest open space. <1504>You create an extra-dimensional hole with a depth of 10 feet per two {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster levels{/g} (maximum 100 feet). At the bottom of the pit is a pool of acid.<br>Any large or smaller creature standing in the area where you first conjured the pit must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} to jump to safety in the nearest open space. In addition, the edges of the pit are sloped, and any creature ending its turn on a square adjacent to the pit must make a Reflex saving throw with a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to avoid falling into it. Creatures subjected to an effect intended to push them into the pit (such as bull rush) do not get a saving throw to avoid falling in if they are affected by the pushing effect.<br>Creatures who fall into the pit take falling {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} as normal ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d6{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Damage_Type}bludgeoning damage{/g} per 10 feet), plus 2d6 points of {g|Encyclopedia:Energy_Damage}acid damage{/g} from per {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} spent in the pit. The walls of the pit are quite slippery: to climb them, a creature needs a successful {g|Encyclopedia:Athletics}Athletics{/g} or {g|Encyclopedia:Mobility}Mobility{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}skill check{/g} with a {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of 30. When the duration of the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} ends, creatures within the hole rise up with the bottom of the pit until they are standing on the surface over the course of a single round.

<1514>You create an extra-dimensional hole with a depth of 10 feet per two {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster levels{/g} (maximum 100 feet). The pit has the ability to squeeze and crush any creature trapped within it.<br>Any large or smaller creature standing in the area where you first conjured the pit must make a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Reflex saving throw{/g} to jump to safety in the nearest open space. In addition, the edges of the pit are sloped, and any creature ending its turn on a square adjacent to the pit must make a Reflex saving throw with a +2 {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to avoid falling into it. Creatures subjected to an effect intended to push them into the pit (such as bull rush) do not get a saving throw to avoid falling in if they are affected by the pushing effect.<br>Creatures who fall into the pit take falling {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} as normal ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d6{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Damage_Type}bludgeoning damage{/g} per 10 feet). In addition, anyone within the pit takes 4d6 points of bludgeoning damage each {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} as the pit contracts and then returns to its normal {g|Encyclopedia:Size}size{/g} (a successful Reflex save halves this damage). The ever-shifting walls of the pit are quite difficult to scale: to climb them, a creature needs a successful {g|Encyclopedia:Athletics}Athletics{/g} or {g|Encyclopedia:Mobility}Mobility{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}skill check{/g} with a {g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} of 35. When the duration of the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} ends, creatures within the hole rise up with the bottom of the pit until they are standing on the surface over the course of a single round.
<1515>You restore life to a deceased party member. A raised creature has a number of {g|Encyclopedia:HP}hit points{/g} equal to its current HD. Raise dead also removes the {g|Encyclopedia:Injury_Death}death{/g}'s door condition.<br>Coming back from the dead is an ordeal. The subject of the {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} gains two permanent negative levels when it is raised, just as if it had been hit by an energy-draining creature, though it can not gain a number of negative levels equal to or greater than its total {g|Encyclopedia:Hit_Dice}Hit Dice{/g} this way.<br>A creature whose permanent negative levels already equal its Hit Dice cannot be brought back to life through raise dead.

<1522>This functions as a targeted dispel magic, but it can dispel one {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} for every four caster levels you possess, starting with the highest level spells and proceeding to lower level spells.<br>Dispel Magic (Target): You can use dispel magic to end one ongoing spell that has been cast on a creature.<br>You make one dispel {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} ({g|Encyclopedia:Dice}1d20{/g} + your {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g}) and compare that to the spell with highest caster level ({g|Encyclopedia:DC}DC{/g} = 11 + the spell's caster level). If successful, that spell ends. If not, compare the same result to the spell with the next highest caster level. Repeat this process until you have dispelled one spell affecting the target, or you have failed to dispel every spell.

<1680>Change Shape (Kitsune)
<1681>With scintillating personalities to match their captivating appearances, gemsoul oreads command attention — and prefer it that way. Gemsouls tend to have large egos and are flashy in their relationships. They gravitate toward artistic pursuits, most frequently of a performative nature, though they also have a strong tradition of symmetric poetry. Gemsouls prefer to make their homes in flamboyant societies or places where they can make meaningful personal contributions, as well as in nations that appreciate cultivated beauty. Gemsouls typically have dazzling, faceted skin, along with a cluster of crystalline formations instead of hair and pupil-less eyes of vivid colors. Some favor tattoos in complex, branching geometric shapes, and they can frequently be seen wearing silks or brightly dyed linen to contrast their complexions.<br>Gemsoul oreads have a +2 racial {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Strength}Strength{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Charisma{/g} and a -2 {g|Encyclopedia:Penalty}penalty{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wisdom{/g}. They can use the color spray {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} once per day.
<1682>Stolid and indomitable, ironsoul oreads undertake great works, both physical and conceptual, with unwavering determination. Possessed of patience and a sense of self-refinement through struggle, ironsouls reflect their ancestry in their continuous and determined efforts toward self-improvement. Ironsouls are most at home in areas that reflect that same sense of directed and intentional growth, and they operate best within existing formal structures. Ironsouls wear their elemental heritage on their skin, with most having some amount of metallic sheen. Their skin tone varies, with colors ranging from gold and silver to copper-green and dull, oxide gray.<br>Ironsoul oreads have a +2 racial {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Constitution}Constitution{/g} and {g|Encyclopedia:Wisdom}Wisdom{/g} and a -2 {g|Encyclopedia:Penalty}penalty{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Dexterity}Dexterity{/g}. They can use the lead blades {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} once per day.

<1693>Sneak {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} is a special power available to some classes (such as arcane trickster, assassin, rogue, slayer, vivisectionist). It allows them to deal more {g|Encyclopedia:Damage}damage{/g} to enemies when they are {g|Encyclopedia:Flat_Footed}flat-footed{/g} or outflanked. You can deal sneak attack damage with both weapons and {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spells{/g}.<br>To deal additional sneak attack damage with a {g|Encyclopedia:RangedAttack}ranged attack{/g} or a spell, you need to be within a 30-foot range from the target. A spell can't deal sneak attack damage unless it requires a ranged {g|Encyclopedia:TouchAttack}touch attack{/g} to hit.
<1694>Lighting
<1695>As your character hides from the superior enemy forces, they will, on numerous occasions, find themselves in dark or dimly lit places. To illuminate an area, you can use a torch or cast a light spell.
<1696>One of your allies is now in disease stage 3. If you don't help them, they will die!<br>{br}Disease stage 3 is a critical condition that requires urgent healing. A character in this stage is too weak to stand for long and they will try to lie down whenever they can.<br>{br}A disease can be cured completely with a scroll of remove disease, alleviated to stage 1 with a potion of restoration, or alleviated by one stage with a potion of lesser restoration.<br>{br}You can check if a character is feeling well by talking to them. Keep track of your allies' condition, or else disease might claim them when you least expect it.
<1697>Diseases
<1698>One of your allies has fallen ill and is now in disease stage 1.<br>During your adventures, your allies may contract dangerous diseases that weaken them. Each time you {g|Encyclopedia:Rest}rest{/g}, all diseases will progress to the next stage. The disease might also worsen when the character encounters various obstacles and trials.<br>{br}The disease scale has five stages, which are reflected in the characters' appearance.<br>At stage 0, your ally is perfectly healthy and their condition does not deteriorate with time.<br>At stage 1, the character is infected, and various negative factors can cause their condition to worsen.<br>At stages 2 and 3, your ally feels ill, and some of the activities that they could previously perform are no longer available to them. Stage 3 signifies a critical condition that requires urgent healing. A character in disease stage 3 is too weak to stand for long and they will try to lie down whenever they can.<br>At stage 4, the character dies.<br>{br}A disease can be cured completely with a scroll of remove disease, alleviated to stage 1 with a potion of restoration, or alleviated by one stage with a potion of lesser restoration.<br>{br}You can check if a character is feeling well by talking to them.
<1699>Interacting With Your Environment
<1700>While playing the Through the Ashes DLC, you might want to pay extra attention to the interactive objects and environment elements that your character encounters.<br>{br}Interacting with them might improve your chances in a fight or even help you entirely avoid combat with particularly dangerous enemies.<br>{br}Hold <b>[{bind|HighlightObjects}]</b> to highlight interactive objects with a color outline and show interaction icons.

<1716>A character assigned to this position can scribe scrolls. To scribe a scroll, he needs to know the required {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g}, have the Scribe Scrolls {g|Encyclopedia:Skills}skill{/g} learned and have all the ingredients, and attempt a {g|Encyclopedia:Lore_Religion}Lore (religion){/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} for a divine spell or a {g|Encyclopedia:Knowledge_Arcana}Knowledge (arcana){/g} check for an arcane spell. It takes time to scribe a scroll. The higher the level of the spell and chosen {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g}, the longer the character will scribe a scroll. The scribing of a scroll takes from 1 to 5 days. If the character passes the check, then the progress of scribing a scroll is advanced by one stage, a failed check does not move the progress.

<1729>During their turn, a character can take a single standard action, a single move action, a single swift action, and an unlimited number of free actions (all these actions together take 6 seconds of the real time). Most often a move action is used for movement or drinking a potion, a standard action for attacks, spellcasting or additional movement, and a swift action for activating special class abilities. Other combinations are also possible. There are also so-called full-round actions, which consume both your standard and move actions. By default, the system chooses an optimum combination of action types to perform your command. You can change this combination using Right Mouse Button.<br>{br}<b>Surprise round</b><br>If you initiate combat with an opponent who is unaware of your presence, your party gets an advantage of a surprise round. During this round, your characters can take a limited number of actions while the opponent cannot act.
<1730><b>{g|Encyclopedia:Move_Action}Move action{/g}.</b><br>As a move {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}action{/g}, a character can move the distance allowed by their {g|Encyclopedia:Speed}speed{/g} or drink a potion. A move action can also be used to change the character's active weapon set. During a {g|Encyclopedia:Surprise}surprise round{/g}, your characters cannot perform a move action.<br>{br}<b>{g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}Standard action{/g}.</b><br>A standard action is used for most combat activities, such as {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacks{/g}, activation of magical items or {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spellcasting{/g}. You can also spend a standard action as a move action.<br>{br}<b>Full-{g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} action.</b><br>This is a long action, which consumes both a standard and a move action of a character in this round (they still can take swift and {g|Encyclopedia:Free_Action}free actions{/g}). It can be used to move a double distance. It can also be used to make a so-called <bs_n1>full attack<bs_n1>: if a character can make more than one attack per round, taking a {g|Encyclopedia:Full_Round_Action}full-round action{/g} allows them to make all of the attacks. Some character abilities (for example, Charge) and spells (such as summon monster) also consume a full-round action.
<1731><b>{g|Encyclopedia:Move_Action}Move action{/g}.</b><br>As a move {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}action{/g}, a character can move the distance allowed by their {g|Encyclopedia:Speed}speed{/g} or drink a potion. A move action can also be used to change the character's active weapon set. During a {g|Encyclopedia:Surprise}surprise round{/g}, your characters cannot perform a move action.<br>{br}<b>{g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}Standard action{/g}.</b><br>A standard action is used for most combat activities, such as {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacks{/g}, activation of magical items or {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spellcasting{/g}. You can also spend a standard action as a move action.<br>{br}<b>Full-{g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} action.</b><br>This is a long action, which consumes both a standard and a move action of a character in this round (they still can take swift and {g|Encyclopedia:Free_Action}free actions{/g}). It can be used to move a double distance. It can also be used to make a so-called <bs_n1>full attack<bs_n1>: if a character can make more than one attack per round, taking a {g|Encyclopedia:Full_Round_Action}full-round action{/g} allows them to make all of the attacks. Some character abilities (for example, Charge) and spells (such as summon monster) also consume a full-round action.
<1732>During their turn, a character can take a single {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g}, a single {g|Encyclopedia:Move_Action}move action{/g}, a single {g|Encyclopedia:Swift_Action}swift action{/g}, and an unlimited number of {g|Encyclopedia:Free_Action}free actions{/g} (all these {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}actions{/g} together take 6 seconds of the <bs_n1>real time<bs_n1>). Most often a move action is used for movement or drinking a potion, a standard action for {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attacks{/g}, {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spellcasting{/g} or additional movement, and a swift action for activating special class abilities. Other combinations are also possible. There are also so-called <bs_n1>full-{g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}round{/g} actions<bs_n1>, which consume both your standard and move actions. By default, the system chooses an optimum combination of action types to perform your command. You can change this combination using <b>{console_bind|Func01}</b>.<br>{br}{g|Encyclopedia:Surprise}<b>Surprise round</b>{/g}<br>If you initiate combat with an opponent who is unaware of your presence, your party gets an advantage of a <bs_n1>surprise round<bs_n1>. During this round, your characters can take a limited number of actions while the opponent cannot act.
<1733>In turn-based combat, characters act in turns. The order of turns is determined by the {g|Encyclopedia:Initiative}initiative{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Check}check{/g} and depends on the character's corresponding {g|Encyclopedia:Ability_Scores}ability score{/g}. A character can act during their turn, and then the turn of the next character in the initiative queue starts.<br>{br}<b>Initiative queue.</b><br>The order of turns is based on the initiative check at the beginning of the combat. The higher the initiative of a character, the lower they are displayed in the queue on the screen and the earlier they act. You can delay your character's turn until a more convenient moment. To do this, use <b>{console_bind|DPadLeft}</b> to open the queue settings, select the character whose turn you want to delay, and press <b>{console_bind|Func01}</b>. Such {g|Encyclopedia:CA_Types}actions{/g} in the initiative queue are irreversible, and the new initiative score is saved for the character until the end of the combat (however, you can still delay the character's turn again).<br>{br}You can hold <b>{console_bind|LeftBottom}</b> to ignore enemies and other objects under the cursor to precisely select a perfect position to move a character.
<1734>Saddle Up
<1735>You can attempt to mount (or dismount, if you're already saddled up) an animal of at least one size category larger than yourself: Large, if you are Medium, or Medium if you are Small. This is a move action.
<1736>Ember gains a +2 morale {g|Encyclopedia:Bonus}bonus{/g} to {g|Encyclopedia:Charisma}Charisma{/g} and a unique 9th level {g|Encyclopedia:Spell}spell{/g} Secret of Serenity.<br>Secret of Serenity: as a {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g}, Ember can use this ability to make all enemies in a 30-foot area become affected by a Secret of Serenity for a number of {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}rounds{/g} equal to her {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}caster level{/g}. Affected creature must pass a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Will saving throw{/g} each round or will be unable to {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} for one round.
<1737>As a {g|Encyclopedia:Standard_Actions}standard action{/g}, Ember can use this ability to make all enemies in a 30-foot area become affected by a Secret of Serenity for {g|Encyclopedia:Caster_Level}Caster Level{/g} {g|Encyclopedia:Combat_Round}rounds{/g}. Affected creature must pass a {g|Encyclopedia:Saving_Throw}Will saving throw{/g} each round or will be unable to {g|Encyclopedia:Attack}attack{/g} for one round.

<1739>You have spent most of you lifetime underground. To survive you've learned to brew potions out of scarce material more efficiently. <1740>Grabber
<1741>Whenever this creature hits an enemy with a melee attack, it also attempts a grapple maneuver check against its target. If the check is successful, the target is grappled.<br>Grappled characters cannot move, and take a -2 penalty on all attack rolls and a -4 penalty to Dexterity-based checks. A grappled character attempts to escape every round by making a successful Athletics or Mobility skill check, or Strength check. The DC of this check is the creature's CMD.<br>Each round, a grappled character suffers damage equal to the grappling creature's attack damage plus its Strength modifier.<br>This ability also gives the creature a +4 bonus on all grapple maneuver checks.
<1742>Grappled
<1743>Grappled characters cannot move, and take a -2 penalty on all attack rolls and a -4 penalty to Dexterity-based checks. A grappled character attempts to escape every round by making a successful Athletics or Mobility skill check, or Strength check. The DC of this check is the creature's CMD.<br>Each round, a grappled character suffers damage equal to the grappling creature's attack damage plus its Strength modifier.
<1744>The creature spits acid at the target. This is a ranged touch attack, the damage it deals is based on the creature's HD. <2027>A treatise by the highborn and powerful ruler, His Excellency the esteemed Count Bartolo Mezinas<br>Westcrown Royal Publishing. Approved by the censors.<br><bs_n1>From time to time, we see some politician unacquainted with warfare but driven by false humanity propose an absurd thought — that wars are somehow supposed to be fought in the open field, away from cities, and only between professional armies so as to avoid casualties among so-called 'non-combatants,' even if they are on the side of the enemy. The supporters of this ludicrous belief must be mistaking war for a tourney where fighters clash in mock battles, their swords clanging against shields as they avoid striking at the stomach or the head for fear of inadvertently delivering a mortal blow — in other words, achieving what would be their goal in real combat. Unlike mock fights, real war is a continuation of politics that utilizes force with the purpose of establishing the dominion of one group of people over another. And since power is the origin point of all morality, it goes without saying that no moral standards that may be customary in everyday life can impose limitations on power struggles — for the victor will be the one who determines those same standards.<br>When one wants to catch fish, one goes to the river, and when one wants to hunt game, ones goes to the forest — the natural habitat of one's prey, where it feeds, breeds, and rests. So if the prey is a human being, where else is one to go if not to the city? The city is where laws are passed, deals are made, treatises are written, and songs are composed. While pursuing the goal of subjugating a hostile society, one must begin by attacking its cities instead of wasting time maneuvering one army around another.<bs_n1>

<2189>Crusade automode
<2190>In this mode, the strategic management of the crusade continues without the player's involvement. Once activated, this mode cannot be deactivated. It has no effect on the main story, but some side quests associated with the crusade might become impossible to complete. The defeat of the crusader armies becomes impossible. Demon forts blocking access to the captured regions will be liberated in stages.
<2191>Crusade morale affects recruitment growth, resource income, and the morale of the crusader armies. Each point of morale above 0 increases the growth of trainable units and resources income by 1%. Each point of morale below 0 reduces the growth of trainable units and resources income by 1%.
<2192>The Military stat represents the level of battle readiness of the crusaders and the general combat effectiveness of the crusader armies.

<2198>Increases AC of all units in a general's army.
<2199>The infirmary replenishes an army's losses after a battle by the number of Value Points equal to its size.
<2200>Movement Points determine the distance an army can move on the global map. If an army runs out of Movement Points, it must rest before it can continue moving.
<2201>When retreating, your army will lose 67% of soldiers and run back to the nearest garrison. The battle will be lost.

<2417>A gowrow, an enormous reptilian beast, had once terrorized the riverside settlements of Sarkoris with its devastating attacks. The locals called it Chillroar for its thick ice-covered hide which no axe or lance could possibly penetrate, and its feral growl could blow a knight off the horse's back. Old, leery, and cunning, the beast satisfied its thirst for blood by destroying anyone who tried to kill it, and there was never a shortage of such brave yet foolish souls. While the small tribe of mountain giants worshiped the ancient gowrow, the Sarkorian tribes considered Chillroar a grim threat, encouraging the best of their warriors to try and defeat it in a feat of strength which would be remembered for ages to come. Despite those heroes' valiant efforts, Chillroar managed to outlive its enemies: Sarkoris fell to the demonic onslaught. The creature's triumph did not last long, however. The opening of the Worldwound had changed the local landscape forever, draining many rivers dry and wrecking the gowrow's lair. Finally, demons tracked down the legendary beast and brutally slayed it.
